title: Fake crypto trading platforms target Australians through WhatsApp and social media groups. Scammers pose as traders in share tip groups, directing victims to fake platforms showing false profits.
Fraudulent letters using official branding claim bank staff are under investigation and instruct recipients to transfer funds to a secure account for asset protection.
Mobile fraud alert issued by regulator and anti-scam centre. Criminals commit fraud by taking control of mobile numbers or making unauthorised account changes to access banking systems.
Investment bond scams impersonate well-known retailers offering environmental bonds with returns up to 9%. Fake websites use legitimate company branding to steal funds and personal data.
Criminals impersonate police officers creating fake crime reports using victims' personal information. Scammers request cryptocurrency transfers claiming accounts are compromised in data breaches.
Construction sector targeted by business email compromise scams. Criminals impersonate suppliers via email to redirect legitimate payments to fraudulent accounts with high-value transactions.
Airline impersonation scams use fake emails and texts claiming refunds, gifts, or expiring points. Messages create urgency and direct victims to phishing websites to steal information.
Social media age restriction exploited in new scams. Messages claim accounts will be suspended or fines issued unless age details are verified through fake websites collecting credentials.
Messaging platform scams use fake billing alerts through guest invitations. Notifications appear from legitimate addresses, bypassing security filters with phone numbers embedded in group names.
Regulator reports over 14,000 investment scam websites taken down. AI washing tactics promise passive income, with scammers using templates, fake corporate documents, and celebrity deepfakes.
Annual report reveals Australians lost $2.18 billion to scams in 2025. Investment scams account for $837.7 million, with losses increasing 7.8% despite stable report volumes.
Fake online stores proliferate on social platforms with too-good-to-be-true prices. Sites steal payment details or send counterfeit goods, targeting shoppers through sponsored advertisements.
